quotes.verbatim_quote:
There are some chords with different names but the same notes, and they may be used in place of
each other.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines synonyms as substitutable enharmonic chord-name equivalents.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The main benefit of knowing chord synonyms is that you can use them to come up with substitute
chords you wouldn’t have come up with otherwise.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the practical purpose of learning synonyms — generating substitutions.

quotes.verbatim_quote: No add 9+ chords are listed; they are the same as 7+ chords whose roots are 2 whole steps higher.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specific synonym rule eliminating an entire category from the reference.

quotes.verbatim_quote: There are more dominant chords, by far, than major or minor types.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Justifies the disproportionate page allocation in the reference charts.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Sometimes 13b9 chords are played with no b7 or no 3rd - not both.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specific tone-omission rule preserving identity of altered dominant.

quotes.verbatim_quote: When the 3rd is left out, this chord is also called a 9 suspended.

quotes.why_load_bearing: States the symmetry property that gives augmented chords 3 names.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This chord is very similar to the 95 chord. The only difference is that the Sth and the +11th (Sth)
are in the +11 chord while just the +1 1th (Sth) is in the 95.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Distinguishes augmented-11th from flat-5 dominant by tone content.

quotes.verbatim_quote: 9b5’s have a less muddy sound and are more easily used
quotes.why_load_bearing: Practical preference for 9b5 over the curious 9#5b5 chord.

quotes.verbatim_quote: There seems to be only one inversion of this amazing chord
quotes.why_load_bearing: Notes the exceptional case of a chord with only one playable inversion.
